questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
One mole of ammonia was completely absorbed in one litre solution each of
(a) \( 1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl} \),
(b) \( 1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \) and
(c) \( 1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \) at \( 298 \mathrm{~K} \).
The decreasing order for the \( \mathrm{pH} \) of the resulting solutions is
(Given \( \mathrm{K}_{b}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)=4.74 \) )
questions.solution:
The reactions involved are as follows:
(I) $\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}$
(III) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
Now, according to $\mathrm{Henderson}$ Haselbach equation
$p \mathrm{H}=p \mathrm{~K}_{a}+\log \left(\frac{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}\right)$
$=14-\mathrm{pK}_{b}+\log \left(\frac{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}\right)$
$=9.26+\log \left(\frac{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}\right)$
Thus, $\mathrm{pH}$ depends on the strength of conjugate acid. The order acid strength of the reactants is as follows:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} < \mathrm{HCl} < \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. Also $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ (aq) is a strong base. As conjugate base of weak acid is strongly acidic, we
have the acidic strength of products as
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4}>\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl} < \left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
That is, (II) $>(\mathrm{I})>(\mathrm{III})$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following shows highest magnetic moment?
questions.solution:
The paramagnetism or magnetic moment produced due to spin of electrons or unpaired electrons is expressed in terms of Bohr magneton (BM) or $\mu_{B}$ and given by Magnetic moment $(\mu)=\sqrt{[n(n+2)]} \mathrm{BM}$ where $n$ is the number of unpaired electrons, in species. Thus, greater is the number of unpaired electrons in an ion greater is its paramagnetic nature.

As $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ion has maximum number of unpaired electrons, thus it has highest magnetic moment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a transition series, with increase in atomic-number, the paramagnetism
options: first increases to a maximum and then decreases
first decreases to a minimum and then increases
questions.solution:
The paramagnetism of transition elements of a given series increases on moving left to right in a period and attain a maximum value and after that, it goes on decreasing towards the end of period. This is due to the fact that the magnetic moment or paramagnetism is directly related to the number of half-filled $d$-orbitals. In the beginning, the number of unpaired electrons in $d$-orbitals increases till the middle of the period $\left(d^{1}\right.$ to $\left.d^{5}\right)$. After this, the pairing of electrons occurs in $d$-orbitals $\left(d^{6}\right.$ to $\left.d^{10}\right)$.
Thus, in a given transition series, with increases in atomic-number, the paramagnetism first increases to a maximum and then decreases.

questions.q: Which one of the following is an example for homogeneous catalysis?
options: Manufacture of sulphuric acid by Contact process
Manufacture of ammonia by Haber's process
Hydrolysis of sucrose in presence of dilute hydrochloric acid
questions.solution:
In homogeneous catalysis, reactants and catalyst are in the same phase. (a) $\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \stackrel{\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{s})}{\rightleftharpoons} \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ (Contact process) It is an example of heterogeneous catalysis. (b) $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \stackrel{\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ (Haber's process) It is an example of heterogeneous catalysis. (c) $\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{aq}) \stackrel{\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq})}{\rightleftharpoons} \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$ $+\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$ It is an example of homogeneous catalysis. (d)

Thus, it is also an example of heterogeneous catalysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During the electrolysis of acidified water, $16$ g of $\text{O}_2$ gas is formed at anode. The volume of $\text{H}_2$ gas liberated at cathode under STP conditions is
questions.solution:
The overall reaction for the electrolysis of acidified water is:

$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$

Number of moles of $\text{O}_2$ produced $= \dfrac{16}{32} = 0.5$ mol

From the balanced chemical equation, $1$ mole of $\text{O}_2$ is produced for every $2$ moles of $\text{H}_2$.

Number of moles of $\text{H}_2$ produced $= 2 \times 0.5 = 1$ mol

At STP, the volume occupied by $1$ mole of an ideal gas is $22.4$ L.

Volume of $\text{H}_2$ gas liberated $= 1 \times 22.4 = 22.4$ L

Answer: $22.4$ L

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4\text{OH})}$ is equal to ____
questions.solution:
According to Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions, the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the limiting ionic conductivities of the cation and the anion.

$\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4\text{OH})} = \lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{OH}^-)}$

Using the limiting molar conductivities of the strong electrolytes $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$, $\text{NaOH}$, and $\text{NaCl}$:

$\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4\text{Cl})} = \lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{Cl}^-)}$

$\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaOH})} = \lambda^0_{m(\text{Na}^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{OH}^-)}$

$\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaCl})} = \lambda^0_{m(\text{Na}^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{Cl}^-)}$

Adding the equations for $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ and $\text{NaOH}$, and subtracting the equation for $\text{NaCl}$:

$\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4\text{Cl})} + \Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaOH})} - \Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaCl})} = (\lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{Cl}^-)}) + (\lambda^0_{m(\text{Na}^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{OH}^-)}) - (\lambda^0_{m(\text{Na}^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{Cl}^-)})$

$\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4\text{Cl})} + \Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaOH})} - \Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaCl})} = \lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4^+)} + \lambda^0_{m(\text{OH}^-)} = \Lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4\text{OH})}$

Answer: $\Lambda^0_{m(\text{NH}_4\text{Cl})} + \Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaOH})} - \Lambda^0_{m(\text{NaCl})}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Given below are the half-cell reactions:
$\text{Mn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mn} \quad (E^0 = -1.18\text{ V})$
$2(\text{Mn}^{3+} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+}) \quad (E^0 = +1.51\text{ V})$
The $E^0_{cell}$ for $3\text{Mn}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Mn} + 2\text{Mn}^{3+}$ will be _____________.
options: $-2.69$ V, the reaction will not occur (Non-Spontaneous)
$-0.33$ V, the reaction will not occur (Non-Spontaneous)
questions.solution:
The given target reaction is $3\text{Mn}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Mn} + 2\text{Mn}^{3+}$

This reaction can be split into two half-reactions:
Cathode (Reduction): $\text{Mn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}$
Anode (Oxidation): $2\text{Mn}^{2+} \rightarrow 2\text{Mn}^{3+} + 2e^-$

The standard reduction potentials are:
$E^0_{\text{cathode}} = E^0_{\text{Mn}^{2+}/\text{Mn}} = -1.18\text{ V}$
$E^0_{\text{anode}} = E^0_{\text{Mn}^{3+}/\text{Mn}^{2+}} = +1.51\text{ V}$

The standard cell potential is given by:
$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{\text{cathode}} - E^0_{\text{anode}}$

$E^0_{cell} = -1.18\text{ V} - 1.51\text{ V} = -2.69\text{ V}$

Since $E^0_{cell}$ is negative, the standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell}$ is positive. Therefore, the reaction is non-spontaneous and will not occur.

Answer: $-2.69$ V, the reaction will not occur (Non-Spontaneous)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The conductivity of centimolar solution of KCl at $298$ K is $0.021\text{ Ohm}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$ and the resistance of the cell containing the solution at $298$ K is $60\,\Omega$. The value of cell constant ($G^*$) is __________.
questions.solution:
The relationship between conductivity ($\kappa$), resistance ($R$), and cell constant ($G^*$) is given by:

$G^* = \kappa \times R$

Substituting the given values:

$\kappa = 0.021 \text{ Ohm}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$

$R = 60 \, \Omega$

$G^* = 0.021 \times 60 = 1.26 \text{ cm}^{-1}$

Answer: $1.26\text{ cm}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many coulombs are required to oxidise 0.1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ to oxygen?
questions.solution:
The reaction involved is a follows
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2+2 e^{-}$
So, from the equation it is clear that 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ required 2 Faradays $=2 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$
So, 0.1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ will require $=\frac{2 \times 96500 \times 0.1}{1}$
$\begin{aligned} & =19300 \mathrm{C} \\ & =1.93 \times 10^4 \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

A current of 3 A is passed through a molten calcium salt for 1 hr 47 min 13 s . The mass of calcium deposited is
(Molar mass of $\mathrm{Ca}=40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
questions.solution:
Given,
$t=1 \mathrm{hr} 47 \mathrm{~min} 13 \mathrm{sec}=6433$ seconds
Molar mass of $\mathrm{Ca}=40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
Current $(i)=3 A$
Mass of calcium deposited $(w)=$ ?
Using Faraday's $2^{\text {nd }}$ law
$w=z i t$     ....(i)
where, $z=\frac{M}{n \times 96500}=\frac{40}{2 \times 96500}$
Substitute the value of $Z$ and given values in equation (i)
$W=\frac{40 \times 3 \times 6433}{2 \times 96500}=3.99 \mathrm{~g} \approx 4 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The resistance of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ weak acid $\mathrm{H} A$ in a conductivity cell is $2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{Ohm}$. The cell constant of the cell is $0.78 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-1}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}$ of acid $\mathrm{H} A$ is $390 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution is
questions.solution:
Given, $C=0.1 \mathrm{M}$
$\begin{aligned} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} & =390 \mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ R & =2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{ohm} \\ G^* & =0.78 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
Now, $K=\frac{R}{G^{\star}}=\frac{0.78}{2 \times 10^3}=3.9 \times 10^{-4}$
$\begin{aligned} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} & =\frac{K \times 1000}{C}=\frac{3.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}{0.1}=3.9 \\ \alpha & =\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}=\frac{3.9}{390}=10^{-2} \\ {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] } & =C \cdot \alpha=0.1 \times 10^{-2}=10^{-3}\end{aligned}$
So, $\quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log 10^{-3}=3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider the following electrodes $P=\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.0001 \mathrm{M}) / \mathrm{Zn}, Q=\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.1$ M) $/ \mathrm{Zn}$ $R=\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) / \mathrm{Zn}, S=\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.001 \mathrm{M}) / \mathrm{Zn}$ $E^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right)=-0.76 \mathrm{~V}$ electrode potentials of the above electrodes in volts are in the order
questions.solution:
The standard potential of $\mathrm{Zn} \mid \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ half-cell $=-0.76 \mathrm{~V}$
Half-cell reaction,
$\overline{\mathrm{Z}} \mathrm{n}^{2 *}(a q)+\overline{\mathcal{2}} e^{=} \longrightarrow \overline{\mathrm{Z}}(s)$
$E_{\text {red }}=E_{\text {red }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}}\right]$
$=-0.76+\frac{0.059}{n} \log \left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]$
Lower the concentration of $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$, lower is the $E_{\text {red }}$ or vice-versa.
Hence, the correct order is $Q>R>S>P$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The resistance of $0.01 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is $1500 \Omega$. If the conductivity of $0.01 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is $0.1466 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. The cell constant of the conductivity cell in $\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ is
questions.solution:
Given, concentration of $\mathrm{KCl}$ solution $=0.01 \mathrm{~m}$ Resistance $(R)$ of $\mathrm{KCl}$ solution $=1500 \Omega$
Conductivity (K) of $0.01 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution at $298 \mathrm{~K}$
$\begin{aligned}
&=0.1466 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \\
&\therefore \text { Cell constant }\left(G^{*}\right)=\kappa R \\
&=0.1466 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 \\
&=0.219
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{AgCl}(s) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Ag}(s)+2 \mathrm{HCl}(a q)$ $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for the cell is $0.22 \mathrm{~V}$. The equilibrium constant at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
For given reaction, $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=0.22 \mathrm{~V}$
At equilibrium,
$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} &=\frac{0.059}{n} \log K_{C} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \log K_{C} &=\frac{E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} \times n}{0.059}=\frac{0.22 \times 2}{0.059}=7.45 \\
K_{C} &=\text { antilog } 7.45 \\
&=2.8 \times 10^{7}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pair of electrolytes that posses same value for the constant $(A)$ in the Debye-Huckel-Onsager equation, $\Lambda_{m}=\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}-A \sqrt{C}$ is
questions.solution:
$\Lambda_{m}=\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}-A \sqrt{C}$
Out of all the given pairs, the pair having same (similar) charges will possess the same value of $A$. Here, $\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$
$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{NaBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$
$\mathrm{NaBr}$ and $\mathrm{MgSO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$
$\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$
So, only in $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{NaBr}$, the charge is same on both the cations and hence, there will have same value of $A$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Given $E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=+0.76 \mathrm{~V}$ and $E_{\mathrm{I}_{2} / \mathrm{I}^{-}}^{\circ}=+0.55 \mathrm{~V}$. The equilibrium constant for the reaction taking place in galvanic cell consisting of above two electrodes is $\left[\frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.06\right]$
questions.solution:
$E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=0.76 \mathrm{~V}$ and $E_{\mathrm{I}_{2} / \mathrm{I}^{-}}^{\circ}=0.55 \mathrm{~V}$ $K=$ ?
Also $\frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.06$
Here, $\mathrm{I}_{2} / \mathrm{I}^{-}$will act as anode and $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ will act as cathode.
$2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{I}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$
Here, $n=2$
$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} &=E_{\text {cathode }}-E_{\text {anode }}=0.76-0.55=+0.21 \\
\Delta G^{\circ} &=-n F E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=-2(96500) \times 0.21 \\
&=-40530 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$
Also, $\Delta G=-2.303 R T \log K_{c}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { So, } \log K_{c} &=\frac{\Delta G}{-\left(\frac{2303 R T}{F}\right) \times F} \\ &=\frac{+40530}{-(0.06) \times 96500}=\frac{40530}{5790} \\ \log K_{c} &=7 \\ K_{c} &=1 \times 10^{7} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The electronegativities of \( \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{Si} \) and \( \mathrm{P} \) are in the order of
questions.solution:
Electronegativity increases along a period and decreases down the group. Nitrogen is expected to have maximum
electronegativity.
Therefore, correct increasing order of electronegativity is \( \mathrm{Si} < \mathrm{P} < \mathrm{C} < \mathrm{N} \).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the manufacture of hydrogen from water gas \( \left(\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}\right) \), which of the following is correct
statement?
options:
\( \mathrm{CO} \) is oxidized to \( \mathrm{CO}_{2} \) with steam in the presence of a catalyst followed by absorption of
\( \mathrm{CO}_{2} \) in alkali.
\( \mathrm{CO} \) and \( \mathrm{H}_{2} \) are separated based on difference in their densities.
\( \mathrm{H}_{2} \) is removed by occlusion with pd.
questions.solution:
In the manufacture of hydrogen from water gas \( \left(\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}\right) \)
\( \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \)
\( \mathrm{CO} \) is oxidized to \( \mathrm{CO}_{2} \) with steam in the presence of a catalyst followed by absorption of \( \mathrm{CO}_{2} \) in alkali.
\( 3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{~N} a \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Bactericidal antibiotics among the following is
questions.solution:
Ofloxacin is bactericidal antibiotic. Erythromycin, tetracycline and chloramphenicol are bacteriostatic antibiotic, that
is, inhibits the growth of organisms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is incorrect in a galvanic cell?
options: The electrode at which electrons are gained is called cathode.
The electrode at which electrons are lost is called cathode.
questions.solution:
The electrode at which electrons are lost is called cathode isincorrect, because electrode at which electrons are lost is
called anode as oxidation, that is, oxidation takes place at anode.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A secondary cell is one
options: can be recharged by passing current through it in the same direction.
can be recharged by passing current through it in the opposite direction.
questions.solution:
In secondary cells electrode reactions can be reversed by external electric energy source, therefore, these cells can
berecharged by passing electric current and used again and again.
The current is passed in the direction opposite to that of the flow of current generated by the cell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The amount of current in Faraday is required for the reduction of \( 1 \mathrm{~mol} \) of \( \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}{ }^{2-} \) ions to
\( C r^{3+} \) is,
questions.solution:
The balance reaction is,
$\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 e^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
From the reaction, $1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}$ requires $6 \mathrm{~F}$ to convert into $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ ion.
$\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{nF}$ (where, $\mathrm{n}=$ no. of $e^{-}$)
$Q=6 \mathrm{~F}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: While charging the lead storage battery,
questions.solution:
Reaction occurring during charging are:
At anode: \( \quad \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(s)+2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}(s)+\mathrm{SO}_{4}{ }^{2-}(a q) \)
At cathode : \( \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{PbO}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{SO}_{4}{ }^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 e^{-} \)
Net reaction: \( 2 \mathrm{PbSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{PbO}_{2}+\mathrm{PbO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \) cannot oxidise
questions.solution:
\( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \) oxidizes \( \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3} \) to \( \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{KI} \) to \( \mathrm{I}_{2} \) and \( \mathrm{PbS} \) to \( \mathrm{PbSO}_{4} \)
But, does not reduces \( \mathrm{O}_{3} \) to \( \mathrm{O}_{2} \) because both are allotropes so, there are no change in oxidation number occurs
\( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In \( \mathrm{H}_{2}-\mathrm{O}_{2} \) fuel cell the reaction occurring at cathode is
questions.solution:
The reaction occurring in \( \mathrm{H}_{2}-\mathrm{O}_{2} \) fuel cells are:
\[ \begin{array}{ll} \text { At anode : } & 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \\ \text {At cathode : } & \mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \end{array} \]
\[ \text { Net reaction : } 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
How many Coulombs of electricity are required for the oxidation of one mol of water to
dioxygen?
questions.solution:
For the oxidation of one mole of water to dioxygen,
\( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 e^{-} \)
\( 2 \mathrm{~F} \) Coulombs of electricity is required.
Therefore, electricity in \( \mathrm{C} \) is
\[ \begin{array}{l} =2 \times F \\ =2 \times 96500 \\ =193000 C \\ =1.93 \times 10^{5} C \end{array} \]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Alkali metals have negative reduction potential and hence they behave as
questions.solution: Standard reduction potential is a measure of tendency of an element to lose electron in aqueous solution. More negative is the reduction potential, higher is the ability of element to lose electrons and hence stronger is the reducing character. Therefore, alkali metals behave as reducing agents.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The emf of a galvanic cell constituted with the electrodes $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Zn}(-0.76 \mathrm{~V})$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Fe}(-0.41 \mathrm{~V})$ is
questions.solution:
The cell reaction may be split into two half reactions as
$\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn} ; \quad E_{\mathrm{red}}^{\circ}=-0.76 \mathrm{~V} \\
&\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe} ; \quad E_{\mathrm{red}}^{\circ}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}$
As $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ ion has lower reduction potential than $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ion. Thus, oxidation takes place on zinc electrode standard EMF of the cell
$=\left[\begin{array}{l}\text { Standard reduction potential of the } \\ \text { the reduction half reaction }\end{array}\right]-$
$\left[\begin{array}{l}\text { Standard reduction potential of } \\ \text { the oxidation half reaction }\end{array}\right]$
$=-0.44-(-0.76)$
$=+0.35 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The equilibrium constant of a reaction is $0.008$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. The standard free energy change of the reaction at the same temperature is
questions.solution:
Standard free energy change,
$\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ} &=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log \mathrm{K} \\ &=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \log 0.008 \\ &=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times(3 \log 2-3 \log 10) \\ &=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times(0.903-3.0) \\ &=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times(-2.097) \\ &=+11965.16 \mathrm{~J}=+11.96 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following has a potential more than zero?
questions.solution:
For Pt, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(1 \mathrm{~atm}) \mid \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{x})$
According to Nernst equation, electrode potential is given by
$$
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \mathrm{x}
$$
(a) For Pt, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(1 \mathrm{~atm}) \mid \mathrm{HCl}(2 \mathrm{M})$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { E cell }_{\text {cell }} &=\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 2 \\
&=\frac{0.0591}{2} \times 0.3010=0.0089
\end{aligned}
$$
(b) For Pt, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(1$ atm $) \mid \mathrm{HCl}(0.1) \mathrm{M}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }} &=\frac{0.0591}{2} \log (0.1) \\
&=\frac{0.0591}{2} \times(-1)=-0.0295
\end{aligned}
$$
(c) For Pt, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(1 \mathrm{~atm}) \mid \mathrm{HCl}(0.5 \mathrm{M})$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }} &=\frac{0.0591}{2} \log (0.5) \\
&=\frac{0.0591}{2}(-0.3010) \\
&=-0.0089
\end{aligned}
$$
(d) For $\mathrm{Pt}, \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ (1 atm) $\mid \mathrm{HCl}$ (1 M)
$$
\mathrm{F}_{\text {cell }}=\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 1=0
$$
Hence, the cell given in option (a) has potential more than zero.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The approximate time duration in hours to electroplate 30 of calcium from molten calcium chloride using a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$ is [At. mass of $\mathrm{Ca}=40$ ]
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}$ (at cathode)
$1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{} \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}=2 \mathrm{~F}$
From $Q=i t$
$\mathrm{n} \cdot 2 \mathrm{~F}=\mathrm{it}$
$t=\frac{n \cdot 2 F}{i}$
$=\frac{30}{40} \times \frac{2 \times 96500}{5}=28950 \mathrm{~s}$
$$
=\frac{28950}{60 \times 60} \mathrm{~h}=8.04 \mathrm{~h}
$$

$\mathrm{F}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=0.0295 \mathrm{~V}$ is $\left[\frac{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{F}}=0.059\right]$

$\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{~B}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~s}) ;$

questions.q: $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ acetic acid is completely neutralized using a standard solution of $\mathrm{NaOH}$. The volume of ethane obtained at STP after the complete electrolysis of the resulting solution is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The standard emf of a galvanic cell involving 2 moles of electrons in its redox reaction is $0.59 \mathrm{~V}$. The equilibrium constant for the redox reaction of the cell is
questions.solution:
In a galvanic cell, $\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}$
Relationship between $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and equilibrium constant
$$
\Delta G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K_{p}
$$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{gathered}
-2.303 R T \log K_{p}=-n F E^{\circ} \\
\log K_{p}=\frac{n F E^{\circ}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{n E^{\circ}}{\frac{2.303 R T}{F}} \\
=\frac{2 \times 0.59}{0.059}=20 \\
K_{p}=\mathrm{AL}(20) \quad(\mathrm{AL}=\text { Antilog }) \\
K_{p}=10^{20}
\end{gathered}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{E}_{1}, \mathrm{E}_{2}, \mathrm{E}_{3}$ are the emf values of the three galvanic cells respectively.
questions.solution:
For the given cell,
$$
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}
$$
(i)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{E}_{1} &=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.1} \\
&=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
(ii) $\mathrm{E}_{2}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{1}$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \times 0
$$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}
$$
(iii) $\mathrm{E}_{3}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{1}$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}+\frac{0.0591}{2}
$$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}_{3}>\mathrm{E}_{2}>\mathrm{E}_{1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The one which decreases with dilution is
questions.solution: The number of ions per cc decreases with dilution and therefore, specific conductance decreases with dilution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following electrolytic solutions has the least specific conductance?
questions.solution:
Specific conductance is defined as the conductance of one centimeter cube (cc) of the solution of an electrolyte. Upon dilution, the concentration of ions per cc decreases, so the specific conductance falls.
Thus, specific conductance $\propto$ concentration of electrolytes, hence specific conductance is least for the solution of $0.002 \mathrm{~N}$ concentration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following gases converts haemoglobin into carboxy haemoglobin?
questions.solution:
Carbon monoxide ( $\mathrm{CO}$ ) converts haemoglobin
to carboxyhaemoglobin. When $\mathrm{CO}$ is inhaled, it binds with haemoglobin in red blood cells with a much higher affinity compared to oxygen.
This binding forms carboxyhaemoglobin which prevents the normal binding of oxygen to. haemoglobin, leading to a decreased oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Prolonged exposure of chloroform in humans may cause damage to liver. It is due to the formation of the following compound.
questions.solution:
The prolonged exposure of chloroform in humans may cause damage to liver. It is because of the formation of phosgene $\mathrm{COCl}_{2}$.
$2 \mathrm{CHCl}_{3}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \stackrel{\text { Sunlight }}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{COCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{HCl}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not a green house gas?
questions.solution: Green house gases are the gases which are forming a layer in the atmosphere and prevent the reflected sun rays going upward and as such are responsible for increasing the temperature. $\mathrm{CFC}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ are green house gases while $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is not a green house gas.

questions.solution:
(A)
Eutrophication refers to excessive increases in minerals and nutrients in aquatic system. Resulting in excess of algae
growth and reduction in dissolved oxygen.

questions.solution:
Hydrogenation of vegetable oil in presence of finely dividednickel as catalyst. The reaction is heterogeneous catalyzes.
Because the phase of the substrate and the catalyst is different.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The intermediates in heteropolar reactions are
questions.solution:
Heteropolar reactions involve the heterolytic cleavage of a covalent bond, where the shared pair of electrons is taken by one of the bonded atoms.

This unequal distribution of electrons results in the formation of charged species, namely a cation (positive ion) and an anion (negative ion).

Therefore, the intermediates formed in heteropolar reactions are both anions and cations.

Answer: Both anions and cations

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Statement-I: Nitrogen in pyridine cannot be estimated by Kjeldahl's method.
Statement-II: Nitrogen in pyridine changes to ammonium sulphate when heated with conc. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ in Kjeldahl's method.
Read the above given statements and choose the correct answer from the given options.
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questions.solution:
Kjeldahl's method is not applicable to compounds containing nitrogen in nitro and azo groups, and nitrogen present in the ring (e.g., pyridine, quinoline).

This is because the nitrogen in these compounds does not change to ammonium sulphate when heated with concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ under the conditions of this method.

Thus, Statement I is true and Statement II is false.

Answer: Statement I is true but Statement II is false

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of chain isomers possible for the hydrocarbon with molecular formula $\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}$ is
questions.solution:
The given molecular formula is $\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}$, which corresponds to an alkane (pentane).

The possible chain isomers are:

1. n-pentane: $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$

2. isopentane (2-methylbutane): $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$

3. neopentane (2,2-dimethylpropane): $\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_4$

Thus, there are $3$ chain isomers possible for $\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}$.

Answer: $3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The compound with molecular formula $\text{C}_{20}\text{H}_{42}$ is
questions.solution:
The given molecular formula is $\text{C}_{20}\text{H}_{42}$.

This matches the general formula for alkanes, $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$, where $n = 20$.

The IUPAC root word for a 20-carbon chain is 'eicos'.

Therefore, the alkane with 20 carbon atoms is named eicosane.

Answer: Eicosane

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond in methyl chloride compared to $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond in chlorobenzene is
questions.solution:
In chlorobenzene, the lone pair of electrons on the chlorine atom is in conjugation with the $\pi$-electron system of the benzene ring. This resonance imparts a partial double bond character to the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond.

In methyl chloride, no such resonance is possible, and the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond is a pure single bond.

Due to the partial double bond character, the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond in chlorobenzene is shorter and stronger than a pure single bond.

Therefore, the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond in methyl chloride is longer and weaker compared to the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond in chlorobenzene.

Answer: Longer and weaker

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct IUPAC name of  is
questions.solution:
To find the correct IUPAC name for the given ether, we follow these steps:

1. Identify the longest continuous carbon chain to serve as the parent alkane. The alkyl groups attached to the oxygen atom are an ethyl group ($-C_2H_5$) and a tert-butyl group ($-C(CH_3)_3$).
2. The longest carbon chain in the tert-butyl group has 3 carbon atoms. Therefore, the parent alkane is propane.
3. Number the parent chain to give the substituents the lowest possible locants. Numbering from either end of the 3-carbon chain gives the central carbon the number 2.
4. Identify the substituents attached to the parent chain at carbon-2:
- A methyl group ($-CH_3$)
- An ethoxy group ($-O-C_2H_5$)
5. Arrange the substituents in alphabetical order. 'Ethoxy' comes before 'methyl'.

Combining these parts, the IUPAC name is 2-ethoxy-2-methylpropane.

Answer: 2-ethoxy-2-methyl propane

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Carboxylic acids are more acidic than phenols because
options: More resonance stabilisation of their conjugate base
questions.solution:
Carboxylic acids dissociate in water to form a carboxylate ion and a proton. The carboxylate ion is highly stabilized by resonance, as the negative charge is delocalized equally over two highly electronegative oxygen atoms, resulting in two equivalent resonance structures.

Phenols dissociate to form a phenoxide ion and a proton. Although the phenoxide ion is also resonance stabilized, the negative charge is delocalized over one oxygen atom and the less electronegative carbon atoms of the benzene ring, resulting in non-equivalent resonance structures.

Since equivalent resonance structures provide greater stability than non-equivalent ones, the carboxylate ion (conjugate base of carboxylic acid) is more resonance stabilized than the phenoxide ion (conjugate base of phenol). Therefore, carboxylic acids are more acidic than phenols.

Answer: More resonance stabilisation of their conjugate base

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Statement I: Staggered conformation of ethane is more stable than the eclipsed conformation.
Statement II: The torsional strain in staggered conformation is more.
Read the above statements and choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questions.solution:
Statement I is true because in the staggered conformation of ethane, the electron clouds of the C-H bonds on adjacent carbon atoms are as far apart as possible. This minimizes the repulsive forces between them, making the staggered conformation more stable than the eclipsed conformation.

Statement II is false because torsional strain arises from the repulsive interaction between the electron clouds of adjacent bonds. In the staggered conformation, this repulsion is at a minimum, so the torsional strain is minimum, not more. It is maximum in the eclipsed conformation.

Therefore, Statement I is true but Statement II is false.

Answer: Statement I is true but Statement II is false

questions.q: Which of the following is the strongest base?
questions.solution:
We know, an anion is strongest base, if its conjugate acid is weakest.
Here, $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$will have conjugate acid $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$.
$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$will have conjugate acid HCl.
$\mathrm{OH}^{-}$will have conjugate acid HOH. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{-}$will have conjugate acid $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$.
Since, out of above four, $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ will be the weakest acid and hence, $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{-}$will be strongest base.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Mesomeric effect involves
questions.solution:
Resonance or mesomeric effect is defined as, the polarity produced in the molecule by the interaction of two $\pi$-bonds or between a $\pi$-bond and a lone pair of electrons present on an adjacent atom. There are two types of mesomeric effect.
$+M$ effect is observed when direction of electron displacement is away from an atom or substituent group attached to the conjugated system e.g., - halogen, $-\mathrm{OH},-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ etc. On the other hand, - M effect is observed when the transfer of electrons is towards the atom or substituent group attached to the conjugted systems, e.g., $-\mathrm{NO}_{2},-\mathrm{COOH}$ etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of optical isomers of the compound $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CHBr}-\mathrm{CHBr}-\mathrm{COOH}$ is
questions.solution:
Compounds having similar physical and chemical properties but differing only in the behaviour towards polarised light are called optical isomers. The number of possible stereoisomers when molecule cannot be divided into two equal halves i.e., molecule has no symmetry. (i) The number of $d$ - and $l$-forms $a=2^{n}$ (where $n=$ number of asymmetric carbon atoms) $\therefore a=2^{n}=2^{2}=4$ Total number of meso forms, $m=0$ Total number of optical isomers $\begin{aligned} &=a+m \\ &=4+0=4 \end{aligned}$

Here (i) and (iii), (i) and (iv), (ii) and (iii), (ii) and (iv) are diastereomeric pairs while (i) and (ii), (iii) and (iv) are enantiomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Meso compounds do not show optical activity because
options: they do not contain chiral carbon atoms
they do not contain plane of symmetry
questions.solution:
Meso compounds are those compounds whose molecules are superimposable on their mirror images inspite of the presence of an asymmetric carbon atom. This is due to internal compensation. The two halves of the molecule rotate the plane of polarised light in opposite direction and hence cancel the effect of each other and make the molecule optically inactive.

Thus, the optical inactivity in meso compounds are because of plane of symmetry or molecular symmetry or internal compensation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Least energetic conformation of cyclohexane is
questions.solution:
Cyclohexane exists in two forms and these are chair and boat conformations. In the chair form, all the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds on adjacent carbon atoms, are in the skew position. In the boat form, four of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds are skew $(1,2 ; 3,4 ; 4,5$ and 6,1$)$ and two are eclipsed $(2,3$ and 5,6$)$. At the same time, there will also be some bond opposition strain for these two pairs of eclipsed bonds and also steric repulsion between the hydrogen pointing towards each other at 1 and 4 .

Hence, the total strain in the boat conformation is larger than that in the chair conformation and consequently the former is less stable than the latter.

questions.solution:
When carbon is bonded to four other atoms, the angle between any pair of bonds $=109^{\circ}, 28^{\prime}$ (tetrahedral angle) but the ring of cyclobutane is square with four angles of $90^{\circ}$.

$$
=19^{\circ} 28^{\prime} / 2=9^{\circ} 44^{\prime}
$$

Calcination involves heating the ore above its melting point.
Smelting involves heating the ore with suitable reducing agent and flux below its melting point.
Calcination of calcium carbonate is endothermic.
questions.solution:
Among the given statements, correct statement is given in option (4). While statement given in options (1), (2) and (3) are incorrect.
Their correct form are as follows
- Roasting involves heating the ore is the regular supply of air.
- Calcination involves heating the ore below its melting point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: For the formation of which compound in Ellingham diagram $\Delta G^{\circ}$ becomes more and more negative with increase in temperature?
questions.solution:
For formation of carbon monoxide ( $\mathrm{CO}$ ), the
Ellingham diagram shows that the $\Delta G^{\circ}$ becomes negative with an increase in temperature. It indicates that the formation of $\mathrm{CO}$ from carbon
(C) and oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ becomes more favourable at higher temperatures.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.q: Copper is extracted from copper pyrites by
questions.solution:
Copper is extracted from copper pyrites by auto reduction process in the following manner.
$\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{CuFeS}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{FeS}+\mathrm{SO}_{2} \\
2 \mathrm{FeS}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} \\
\mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{SiO}_{2} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{FeSiO}_{3} \\
2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} \\
2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}
\end{aligned}$

questions.solution:

The acid strength of active methylene group in
(a) \( \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{2} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \)

questions.solution:
An active methylene compound is a compound that has the following general structural formula.

Where, \( E_{1} \) and \( E_{2} \) are functional group that withdraws electron by resonance. After the removal of hydrogen from the

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: From the Ellingham graphs on carbon, which of the following statements is false?
options: $\mathrm{CO}$ is less stable than $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ at more than $983 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{CO}$ reduces $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ to $\mathrm{Fe}$ in the reduction zone of blast furnace
$\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is more stable than $\mathrm{CO}$ at less than $983 \mathrm{~K}$
questions.solution: In Ellingham graph, initially the $\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ more negative for $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, thus, $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is more stable than CO but when the temperature becomes $983 \mathrm{~K}$, the conditions inverted i.e., $\Delta_{f} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ of $\mathrm{CO}$ becomes more negative and hence above $983 \mathrm{~K}$, CO becomes more stable than $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.q: In the electrolytic refining of zinc
options: the impure metal is at the cathode
the metal ion get reduced at the anode
acidified zinc sulphate is the electrolyte
questions.solution:
In the electrolytic refining of zinc, anode is made up of impure zinc while a strip of pure zinc acts as cathode. An acidified solution of zinc sulphate acts as electrolyte. When electricity is passed, following reactions occur
At cathode
$$
\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { pure }}{\mathrm{Zn}}
$$
At anode
$$
\underset{\text { impure }}{\mathrm{Zn}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: In chromite ore, the oxidation number of iron, and chromium are respectively
questions.solution:
Chromite or chrome iron ore is $\mathrm{FeCr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. Its actual composition is $\mathrm{FeO} \cdot \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.
In $\mathrm{FeO}$, oxidation state of $\mathrm{Fe}$ is calculated as
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{x}+(-2) &=0 \\
\mathrm{x} &=+2
\end{aligned}
$$
Similarly, in $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, the oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$ is calculated as
$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \times x+(-2) \times 3 &=0 \\
2 x &=+6 \\
x &=+3
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: Carbon can reduce ferric oxide to iron at a temperature above $983 \mathrm{~K}$ because
options: carbon monoxide formed is thermodynamically less stable than ferric oxide
carbon has a higher affinity towards oxidation than iron
free energy change for the formation of carbon dioxide is less negative than that for ferric oxide
iron has a higher affinity towards oxygen than carbon
questions.solution: Above $983 \mathrm{~K}$, free energy change for the formation of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is more negative than that for ferric oxide. Thus, above this temperature, carbon has a higher affinity towards oxidation than iron.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The compound from which chlorobenzene cannot be prepared easily is
questions.solution:
Chlorobenzene can be easily prepared from aniline by first converting it to benzene diazonium chloride, followed by the Sandmeyer reaction.

Chlorobenzene can be easily prepared from benzene by electrophilic aromatic substitution using $Cl_2$ in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst like $FeCl_3$ or $AlCl_3$.

Chlorobenzene can be easily prepared from benzene diazonium chloride by treating it with $Cu_2Cl_2$ and $HCl$ (Sandmeyer reaction).

In phenol, the carbon-oxygen bond acquires a partial double bond character due to resonance between the lone pairs on oxygen and the benzene ring. This makes the $C-O$ bond stronger and difficult to cleave. Therefore, nucleophilic substitution of the hydroxyl group by a chloride ion (using reagents like $PCl_5$ or $SOCl_2$) is very difficult, and chlorobenzene cannot be prepared easily from phenol.

Answer: Phenol

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In $\text{S}_\text{N}1$ reaction, the alkyl halide that on hydrolysis produces racemic mixture is
questions.solution:
In an $\text{S}_\text{N}1$ reaction, the formation of a racemic mixture occurs only if the product formed has a chiral center.

Tertiary butyl bromide on hydrolysis gives tertiary butyl alcohol, which is achiral.

2-bromobutane on hydrolysis forms a planar secondary carbocation intermediate. The nucleophile (water) can attack from either face of the planar carbocation with equal probability, leading to the formation of $d$ and $l$ enantiomers of 2-butanol. Since 2-butanol has a chiral carbon, a racemic mixture is produced.

Isopropyl bromide gives isopropyl alcohol, which is achiral.

Methyl bromide gives methyl alcohol, which is achiral.

Therefore, 2-bromobutane produces a racemic mixture.

Answer: 2-bromobutane

$\mathrm{Cl}$ in compound $B$ is substituted by $\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ to give compound $C\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{~N}\right)$.

$C$ gives a diazonium salt with $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}$ which on further treatment with water give an optically active alcohol. That means $C$ is an aliphatic amine.

From the given information it is concluded that the most probable structure of $A$ is $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$. As only but-2-ene gives optically active alcohol $(D)$ as final product. The reactions involves are as follows.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The order of reactivity of the compounds $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right) \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{Br}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right) \mathrm{Br}$ in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reaction is
questions.solution:
The order of reactivity of given compounds in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}$ 2 reaction is

For $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reaction, the order of reaction is inversely proportional to steric hinderance cause by bulky groups. In

there are two bulky groups are present i.e. benzene group and one methyl group which provide steric hinderance for the attack of nucleophile. Hence, it is least reactive while in $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}\left(1^{\circ}\right.$ halide ), the attack of nucleophile is easier due to less steric hinderance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following halide shows highest reactivity towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction?
questions.solution:
The order of reactivity of halides for $S_{N} 1$ reaction is $3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ}$ as the stability of carbocation formed in $S_{N} 1$ will be highest for $3^{\circ}$ and lowest for $1^{\circ}$. Here, $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}$ will show highest reactivity for $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism as the carbocation left after removel of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$is resonance stablised.

So, $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}$ will show highest reactivity towards $S_{N} 1$ reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not true for $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}$ l reaction?
options: The rate of the reaction does not depend upon the molar concentration of the nucleophile
It is favoured by polar solvents
questions.solution:
In $S_{N} 1$ reactions, rate of the reaction depends upon the concentration of the substrate only. It does not depend upon the concentration of the nucleophile.
In this type of reaction, covalent nucleophile reacts/attacks on substrate. $3^{\circ}$ alkyl halide generally react by this mechanism, whereas $1^{\circ}$ alkyl halide generally follow $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reactions. $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reactions are favoured by polar solvents.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction, the nitro group is meta directing because it
options: decreases electron density at ortho and para positions
decreases electron density at meta position
increases electron density at meta position
increases electron density at ortho and para positions
questions.solution: When nitro group is present in the benzene nucleus, it withdraws electrons from $o$ and $p$-positions. Thus, the electron density at the $o$ and $p$-positions decreases. $m$-positions become positions of comparatively higher electron density and therefore, electrophilic attack occurs at $m$-positions.

$$
\mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{SOCl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{RCl}+\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}
$$
The other products being gases escape leaving behind pure alkyl halide.

questions.subject: Chemistry

options: chlorination followed by the reaction with alcoholic KOH
chlorination followed by the reaction with aqueous $\mathrm{KOH}$
questions.solution: The chlorination of methane is carried out by treating it with chlorine in presence of ultraviolet light or by heating the reaction mixture to $520-670 \mathrm{~K}$. $\underset{\text { methane }}{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \stackrel{\text { hv or } \Delta}{\longrightarrow} \underset{\text { methyl chloride }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}}+\mathrm{HCl}$ This methyl chloride react with metallic $\mathrm{Na}$ in presence of dry ether to form symmetrical alkane the number of carbon atoms (ethane). This reaction is called Wurtz reaction.

questions.solution: Benzene on treatment with a carboxylic acid chloride or anhydride in the presence of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ forms aliphatic-aromatic ketones. The reaction is known as Friedel-Crafts acylation.

questions.solution:
Alkyl halides react with metallic Na in presence of dry ether to form symmetrical alkanes containing double the number of carbon atoms present in the alkyl halide.

This reaction is called Wurtz reaction.

questions.solution: Dry distillation of mixtue of calcium acetate and calcium formate gives acetaldehyde.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An organic compound ' $A$ ' burns with a sooty flame. It is negative towards Tollen's reagent test and positive for Borsche's reagent test. The compound ' $A$ ' is
questions.solution: Since given compound 'A' burns with sooty flame, therefore it must be an aromatic compound. Again when it reacts with Borsche's reagent (acidic 2, 4-dinitrophenyl hydrazine solution), it yields orange precipitate. Therefore it must be a carbonyl compound, i.e., aldehyde, or ketone. But 'A' does not perform Tollen's reagent test therefore, it must be an aromatic ketone. Hence, 'A' is acetophenone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $9.65 \mathrm{C}$ of electric current is passed through fused anhydrous magnesium chloride. The magnesium metal thus, obtained is completely converted into a Grignard reagent. The number of moles of the Grignard reagent obtained is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{MgCl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
$\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \underset{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{\mathrm{Mg}}$ (at cathode)
$\because 2 \mathrm{~F}(2 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}) \text { deposits } \mathrm{Mg}=1 \mathrm{~mol}$
$\therefore 9.65 \mathrm{C}(2 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$ ) deposits $\mathrm{Mg}=1 \mathrm{~mol}$
$=\frac{1 \times 9.65}{2 \times 96500}$
$=5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol}$
In order to prepare Grignard reagent, one mole of $\mathrm{Mg}$ is used per mole of reagent obtained. Thus, by $5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{Mg}, 5 \times 10^{-5}$ mole of Grignard reagent are obtained.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In order to increase the volume of a gas by $10 \%$, the pressure of the gas should be
questions.solution:
According to Boyle's law,
$$
\mathrm{p} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~V}}
$$
Hence, in order to increase the volume of a gas by $10 \%$, the pressure of the gas should be decreased by $10 \%$.

Oils $+\mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow$Dalda

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \) is
questions.solution:
\( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \) can act as both oxidizing as well as reducing agent in both acidic and basic media.
Oxidizing action:
In acidic media,
\( 2 \mathrm{~F} e^{2+}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~F} e^{3+}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \)
In basic media, \( 2 \mathrm{~F} e^{2+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~F} e^{3+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \)
Reducing action:
In acidic media,
\( \mathrm{HOCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \)
In basic media,
\( \mathrm{I}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following ions will cause hardness in water?

\( C-1 \) of \( \alpha- \) glucose and \( C-4 \) of \( \beta \)-fructose

The components of sucrose, that is, glucose and fructose arelinked via an acetal bond formed between \( \mathrm{Cl} \) on the \( \alpha- \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Hydrogen gas is not liberated when the following metal is added to dil $\mathrm{HCl}$
questions.solution:
The metals, present below hydrogen in the electrochemical series, cannot liberate hydrogen from the dilute acids.
Among the given metals only $\mathrm{Ag}$ is present below hydrogen in electrochemical series, so it does not evolve hydrogen with dil HCl.
$$
\mathrm{Ag}+\text { dil } \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \text { No reaction }
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A $0.15$ mole of pyridinium chloride has been added to $500\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.2$ M pyridine solution (a base). Assuming there is no change in volume upon mixing(Given: $pK_b$ of pyridine = 8.82), the pH of the resulting solution is
questions.solution:
Moles of pyridine (base) $= 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.1$ mol

Moles of pyridinium chloride (salt) $= 0.15$ mol

Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a basic buffer:

$pOH = pK_b + \log\left(\dfrac{\text{Moles of salt}}{\text{Moles of base}}\right)$

$pOH = 8.82 + \log\left(\dfrac{0.15}{0.10}\right)$

$pOH = 8.82 + \log(1.5)$

$pOH = 8.82 + 0.176 \approx 9$

$pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 9 = 5$

Answer: $5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Solubility product of $\mathrm{CaC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ at a given temperature in pure water is $4 \times 10^{-9}\left(\mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}\right)^2$. Solubility of $\mathrm{CaC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ at the same temperature is
questions.solution:
Given,
$K_{\text {sp }}=4 \times 10^{-9}\left(\mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}\right)^2$
$S=$ ?
$\mathrm{CaC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-}$
$K_{\mathrm{sp}}=(S)(S)$
$4 \times 10^{-9}=S^2$
$\Rightarrow \quad S=\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-9}}$
$=6.3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
Thus, the solubility of $\mathrm{CaC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ is $6.3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A weak acid with $\mathrm{p} K_a 5.9$ and weak base with $\mathrm{p} K_b 5.8$ are mixed in equal proportions. $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resulting solution is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{pH}$ of the resultant solution is given by,
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{pH} & =7+\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{pK}_a-\mathrm{p} K_b\right)=7+\frac{1}{2}(5.9-5.8) \\ \mathrm{pH} & =7.05\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which among the following has highest $\mathrm{pH}$ ?
questions.solution:
For acidic medium, we know that $\mathrm{pH}$ values range is between $0-6.9$, for neutral it is 7 and for bases it is $7.1-14$. So, $\mathrm{HCl}$ is acidic and $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is basic. Thus, we can straight forward infer that $\mathrm{NaOH}$ has a higher $\mathrm{pH}$ value.
Now, 1 mole of sodium hydroxide produces 1 mole of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and 1 mole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions. This means that the concentration of the hydroxide ions will be equal to $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=[\mathrm{NaOH}]=1 \mathrm{M}$
For case $\mathrm{A}$,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=-\log [1]=0 \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-0=14 \\
& \text { For case } B \text {, } \\
& {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=[\mathrm{NaOH}]=0.1 \mathrm{M}} \\
& \text { pOH }=-\log [0.1]=1 \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-1=13 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
So, pH of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ is highest among the given solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $K_{a}$ values for acids $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{3}, \mathrm{HNO}_{2}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ and $\mathrm{HCN}$ are respectively $1.3 \times 10^{-2}$, $4 \times 10^{-4}, 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ and $4 \times 10^{-10}$, which of the above acids produces stronger conjugate base in aqueous solution?
questions.solution:
Lower the value of $K_{a}$, lower will be the acidic strength and stronger will be the conjugate base. Therefore, among the given acids, HCN has the lowest $K_{a}$ value (i.e. $4 \times 10^{-10}$ ).
Hence, it is the strongest conjugate base.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The conjugate base of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is
questions.solution:
The conjugate base of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ can be obtained after removal of one hydrogen ion.
$\mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$
So, $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$is the conjugate base of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.

\( \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{OH})_{3} \) functions as

Boric acid is a weak mono basic acid (not a protonic acid) but acts as a Lewis acid by accepting electrons from \( O H^{-} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The aqueous solution of following salt will have the lowest \( \mathrm{pH} \) :
questions.solution:
The pH of the aqueous solution of the following salts depend on corresponding acid.
$\mathrm{NaClO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{HClO}$
$\mathrm{NaClO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$
$\mathrm{NaClO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{HClO}_{3}$
$\mathrm{NaClO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{HClO}_{2}$
$\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ is the strongest acid, therefore, its salt solution will have the lowest pH value.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The correct arrangement of the species in the decreasing order of the bond length between
carbon and oxygen in them is
questions.solution:
We know Bond order \( \propto \frac{1}{\text { Bond length }} \)
CO molecule have \( 3 \) bonds between \( C \) and \( O \).
\[ C=O \]
\( \mathrm{CO}_{2} \) molecule have \( 2 \) double bonds between \( \mathrm{C} \) and \( \mathrm{O} \)
\[ O=C=O \]
\( \mathrm{CO}_{3}{ }^{2-} \) ion have \( 3 \) partial double bonds between \( \mathrm{C} \) and \( \mathrm{O} \), due to resonance.

\( \mathrm{HCO}_{2}{ }^{-} \)ion also show resonance, but due to the presence of hydrogen atom attached to carbon atom it has less
resonance hybrid, therefore, \( \mathrm{HCO}_{2}{ }^{-} \)ion have greater bond order than \( \mathrm{CO}_{3}{ }^{2-} \)

Hence, the correct decreasing order of bond length is \( \mathrm{CO}_{3}{ }^{2-}, \mathrm{HCO}_{2}{ }^{-}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO} \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A crystalline solid \( X Y_{3} \) has ccp arrangement for its element \( Y . X \) occupies
questions.solution:
\( X Y_{3} \) has ccp arrangement for element \( Y, X \) occupies \( 33 \% \) octahedral voids.
In case of close packed arrangement there is one octahedral and two tetrahedral voids corresponding to each atom
constituting the lattice.
Atom \( Y \) adopt ccp arrangement and there are \( 2 \) tetrahedral sites per atom of \( Y \). Atom \( X \) occupies one octahedral void
corresponding to each atom constituting the lattice. Total there are \( 3 \) voids
In \( \% \times \) occupies \( \frac{1}{3} \times 100 \% \) octahedral voids \( =33.33 \% \) octahedral voids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An example for a neutral buffer is
questions.solution: Acetic acid and sodium acetate, citric acid and sodium citrate are acidic buffers, whereas ammonium hydroxide and ammonium chloride is a basic buffer solution. A neutral buffer is a solution of weak acid (acetic acid) and weak base (ammonium hydroxide).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Conjugate base of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$is
questions.solution:
A conjugate pair of acid and a base differs by a proton only, i.e.,
conjugate acid $\rightleftharpoons$ conjugate base $+\mathrm{H}^{+}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}$
Thus, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$can give a proton and hence is an acid while $\mathrm{HPO}_{4}^{2-}$ can accept the proton and hence is a base.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify a species which is not a Bronsted acid but a Lewis acid
questions.solution: Bronsted defined an acid as a substance, which has a tendency to give a proton $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$, on the other hand, Lewis proposed an acid as a substance, which is capable of accepting a pair of electrons. $\mathrm{As} \mathrm{}_{3} \mathrm{H}^{+}$, $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$ has $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions, these are Bronsted acids whereas $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is an electron deficiet molecule, thus be have like a Lewis acid, i.e., electron pair acceptor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of $10^{-8} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$ solution is
questions.solution:
As $\mathrm{HCl}$ solution is acidic in nature and the $\mathrm{pH}$ of an acidic solution cannot exceed more than 7 . Thus, the answer must be $6.9586$.
We can also find this answer through calculations. From acid, $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-8} \mathrm{M}$
But the $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$ions from water, i.e., $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-7} \mathrm{M}$ cannot be neglected in comparison to $10^{-8} \mathrm{M}$. The $\mathrm{pH}$ can be calculated as follows
From acid, $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-8} \mathrm{M}$
From water, $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-7} \mathrm{M}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \text { Total }\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] &=10^{-8}+10^{-7}=10^{-8}(1+10) \\
&=11 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{M} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH} &=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\
&=-\log \left(11 \times 10^{-8}\right) \\
&=-\left[\log 11+\log 10^{-8}\right] \\
&=-[1.0414-8]=6.9586
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $5 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.4 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{NaOH}$ is mixed with $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{HCl}$. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resulting solution will be
questions.solution:
From the first law of thermodynamics, the total energy of the universe remains constant.
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Milliequivalents of } \mathrm{NaOH}=\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1} \\
&\qquad=0.4 \times 5=0.20 \\
&\text { Milliequivalents of } \mathrm{HCl}=\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2} \\
&\qquad=0.1 \times 20=0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On adding which of the following, the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{HCl}$ will not alter?
questions.solution:
Milliequivalents of $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{HCl}=\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}$
$$
=20 \times 0.1=2
$$
(a) $\mathrm{pH}$ of distilled water $=7$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-\mathrm{pH}}=& 10^{-7} \mathrm{M} \\
\therefore \text { milliequivalents } &=\mathrm{NV} \quad \text { [for } \mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{N}=\mathrm{M} \text { ] } \\
&=10^{-7} \times 20=2.0 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$
(b) Milliequivalents of $\mathrm{NaOH}=0.1 \times 1=0.1$
(c) $\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}=1$
$\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-1}=0.1 \mathrm{M}$
$\therefore$ Milliequivalents $=0.1 \times 500=50$
(d) Milliequivalents of $\mathrm{HCl}=1 \times 1=1$
$\because$ Milliequivalents of $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ having $\mathrm{pH}=1$ is more than that of $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{HCl}$, therefore adding this, $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{HCl}$ solution, does not alter.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution obtained by mixing $100 \mathrm{ml}$ of a solution of $\mathrm{pH}=3$ with $400 \mathrm{~mL}$ of a solution of $\mathrm{pH}=4$ is
questions.solution:
For solution $\mathrm{I}, \mathrm{pH}=3$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-3}
$$
$\Rightarrow$ Concentration of solution $\mathrm{M}_{1}=10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$
$$
\mathrm{V}_{1}=100 \mathrm{~mL}
$$
For solution II, pH = 4
$\Rightarrow \quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-4}$ $\Rightarrow$ Concentration of solution, $\mathrm{M}_{2}=10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{V}_{2}=400 \mathrm{~mL}$
Concentration of resulting solution
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{M} &=\frac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}+\mathrm{V}_{2}} \\
&=\frac{10^{-3} \times 100+10^{-4} \times 400}{100+400} \\
&=\frac{0.14}{500} \\
\mathrm{M} &=0.00028=2.8 \times 10^{-4} \\
{\left[\quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\right.} &=2.8 \times 10^{-4} \\
\therefore \mathrm{pH} \text { of resulting solution } \\
&=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\
&=-\log \left(2.8 \times 10^{-4}\right) \\
&=-\log 2.8-\log 10^{-4} \\
&=4-\log 2.8
\end{aligned}
$$

[acid] $=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{a}+\log \frac{[0.2]}{[0.1]}$
$\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log 2$

questions.q:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ is passed into one $\mathrm{nm}^{3}$ of a solution containing $0.1$ mole of $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ and $0.01$ mole of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ till the sulphide ion concentration reaches to $8.1 \times 10^{-19}$ moles. Which one of the following statements is true?

questions.solution: The ionic product for $\mathrm{ZnS}\left(0.1 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-19}\right)$ and for CuS $\left(0.01 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-19}\right)$ exceeds their $K_{\mathrm{sp}}$ values. Hence, both get precipitated.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $0.023 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium metal is reacted with $100 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of water. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resulting solution is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{Na}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
$(2 \times 23=46) \quad(2 \times 40=80)$
$0.023 \quad 0.04$
${\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\frac{0.04}{40} \times 10=10^{-2} }$
$\because \quad \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$\mathrm{pOH}=2$
$\because \quad \mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14$
$\mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}$
$\mathrm{pH}=14-2$
$\mathrm{pH}=12$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $0.023 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium metal is reacted with $100 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of water. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resulting solution is
questions.solution: Given, $\frac{0.023}{23}$ mol $\frac{100}{22400} \mathrm{~mol}$ $=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}=4.46 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$ Thus, Na is the limiting reagent and decide the amount of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ formed. $\because 1$ mole Na give $\mathrm{NaOH}=1 \mathrm{~mol}$ $\therefore 1 \times 10^{-3}$ mole Na will give $\mathrm{NaOH}$ $=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$ Concentration of $$ \begin{aligned} {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] } &=\frac{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{100}=1 \times 10^{-2} \\ \mathrm{pOH} &=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\ &=-\log \left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right) \\ &=2 \\ \mathrm{pH} &=14-2=12 \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A buffer solution contains $0.1$ mole of sodium acetate in $1000 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ acetic acid. To the above buffer solution, $0.1$ mole of sodium acetate is further added and dissolved. The $\mathrm{pH}$ of the resulting buffer is equal to
questions.solution:
Initially $\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]=0.1 \mathrm{~mol}$
$\left[\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}\right]=0.1 \mathrm{~mol}$
when $0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}$ is added.
$$
\begin{aligned}
{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right] } &=0.1+0.1=0.2 \mathrm{~mol} \\
\mathrm{pH} &=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{\text { [salt }]}{\text { [acid] }} \\
&=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{0.2}{0.1} \\
&=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log 2
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$1 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ solution containing $10^{-5}$ moles each of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions and $\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{2-}$ ions is treated with $10^{-4}$ moles of silver nitrate. Which one of the following observations is made?
$\begin{aligned} {\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}} \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}\right.} &\left.=4 \times 10^{-12}\right] \\ {\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}} \mathrm{AgCl}\right.} &\left.=1 \times 10^{-10}\right] \end{aligned}$
options: Both silver chromate and silver chloride start precipitating simultaneously
questions.solution:
For precipitation,
ionic product $>$ solubility product $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}\right)$
For $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { ionic product } &=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{-}\right] \\
&=\left(10^{-4}\right)^{2}\left(10^{-5}\right)=10^{-13}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}=4 \times 10^{-12}$
Here, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}>\mathrm{IP}$
Thus, no precipitate is obtained.
For $\mathrm{AgCl}$,
ionic product $=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]$
$=\left[10^{-4}\right]\left[10^{-5}\right]$
$=10^{-9}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{AgCl})=1 \times 10^{-10}$
Here, IP > $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$
So, precipitate will form.
Thus, silver chloride gets precipitated first.

questions.q: $30 \operatorname{cc}$ of $\frac{\mathrm{M}}{3} \mathrm{HCl}, 20 \operatorname{cc}$ of $\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and $40 \mathrm{cc}$ of $\frac{\mathrm{M}}{4} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solutions are mixed and the volume was made up to $1 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$. Milli equivalence of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$left

=20-10=10 \\

\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride solution, which of the half cell reaction will occur
at anode?
options: \( N a^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \rightarrow N a(s) ; E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=-2.71 \) volts
questions.solution:
In water sodium chloride ionizes as
\( N a C l(a q) \rightleftharpoons N a^{+}(a q)+C l^{-}(a q) \)
water also dissociates into ions, though to very slight degree as,
\( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \)
Thus, the aqueous solution of sodium chloride contains \( \mathrm{N} a^{+}, \mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{OH}^{-} \)and \( \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \)ions. When electric current is passed
through the solution, \( \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \) gas evolved at anode and hydrogen is evolved at the cathode. The resulting solution contains
\( \mathrm{Na}^{+} \)and \( \mathrm{OH}^{-} \)ions. Oxidation of \( \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \)ions and \( \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \) is possible at anode but, standard electrode potential of \( \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \)is
more, therefore, \( \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \)will oxidized at anode.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Hybridised states of carbon in graphite and diamond are respectively
questions.solution: In graphite, carbon is $s p^{2}$ hybridised. Each carbon is thus linked to three other carbon atoms forming hexagonal rings. On the other hand, in diamond carbon is $s p^{3}$ hybridised. Each carbon is tetrahedrally linked to four strong C-C, $s p^{3}-s p^{3} \sigma$-bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An organic compound on heating with $\mathrm{CuO}$ produces $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ but no water. The organic compound may be
questions.solution: Since, the compound on heating with $\mathrm{CuO}$ produced $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, it contains carbon. Again, it does not produce water, hence it does not contain hydrogen. So, the organic compound is carbon tetrachloride $\left(\mathrm{CCl}_{4}\right)$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When carbon monoxide is passed over solid caustic soda heated to $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, it forms
questions.solution:
When carbon monoxide is passed over solid caustic soda at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, sodium formate is obtained.
$\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{NaOH} \stackrel{200^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / 10 \mathrm{~atm}}{\longrightarrow} \underset{\text { Sodium formate }}{\mathrm{HCOONa}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Graphite is a soft solid lubricant extremely difficult to melt. The reason for this anomalous behaviour is that graphite
options: is an allotropic form of carbon
has carbon atoms arranged in large plates of rings of strongly bond carbon atoms with weak interplate bonds
has molecules of variable molecular masses like polymers
questions.solution: Graphite is a soft solid lubricant extremely difficult to melt. The reason for this anomalous behaviour is that graphite has carbon atoms arranged in large plates of rings of strongly bound carbon atoms with weak interplate bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following nitrate will decompose to give $\mathrm{NO}_2$ on heating ?
questions.solution:
Among the given nitrates, only $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ will give $\mathrm{NO}_2$ on heating. While all other nitrates will give oxygen.
i.e. $\mathrm{LiNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{O}+4 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$
$2 \mathrm{MNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{MNO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$
(where, $M=\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{K}, \mathrm{Rb}$ )

questions.solution: Among the given halides, only $\mathrm{CCl}_4$ cannot be hydrolysed due to absence of vacant $d$-orbitals.

questions.solution:
Statement given in option (4) is incorrect while all other statements are correct.
The correct form of option (4) is ozone absorbs UV radiation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct decreasing order of boiling point of hydrogen halides is
questions.solution: HF is hydrogen bonded, thus it has highest boiling point. The remaining hydrogen halides are gaseous and their boiling points depend on the van der Waals' forces. Greater the molecule mass, greater will be the van der Waals' forces. Hence higher is the boiling point. Thus, the correct order is $\mathrm{HF}\gt\mathrm{HI}\gt\mathrm{HBr}\gt\mathrm{HCl}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A pair of amphoteric oxides is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{BeO}, \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ and $\mathrm{ZnO}$ are amphoteric in nature
$\mathrm{MgO}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{O}$ are basic in nature whereas $\mathrm{BO}_3$ is acidic. Thus, the pair of amphoteric oxides are $\mathrm{BeO}$ and $\mathrm{ZnO}$.

questions.solution:
Among the given compounds, only
ammonium nitrate does not produces $\mathrm{N}_2$ gas on decomposition.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{N}_3\right)_2 \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{Ba}+3 \mathrm{~N}_2 \\ & \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_2 \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{N}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3 \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+\mathrm{N}_2+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct decreasing order of basicity of hydrides of group- 15 elements is
questions.solution:
In general, the basicity of group 15 hydrides
decreases on moving down the group from $\mathrm{NH}_3$ to $\mathrm{SbH}_3$. This is due to the increasing size of the central atom and the decreasing availability of lone pair for donation. Thus, the correct order of basicity of hydrides is
$\mathrm{NH}_3>\mathrm{PH}_3>\mathrm{AsH}_3>\mathrm{SbH}_3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following oxoacids of phosphorus can reduce $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ to metallic silver?
questions.solution:
Hypophosphorus acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_2\right)$ is a strong
reducing agent which can effectively reduce $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ ions to $\mathrm{Ag}$ and itself gets oxidise to phosphorus acid $\left[\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3\right]$ as follows
$\begin{aligned} 3 \mathrm{AgNO}_3+4 \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow & 3 \mathrm{Ag}+4 \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3 \\ & +3 \mathrm{HNO}_3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The property of halogens which is not correctly matched is
questions.solution: Down the group, electron gain enthalpies become less and less negative with the increase in the size of the halogen. However, electron gain enthalpy of $\mathrm{F}$ is less negative than $\mathrm{Cl}$. This is due to small size of F-atom. As a result, there are strong inter electronic repulsions in the relatively small $2 p$-orbitals of $\mathrm{F}$ and thus incoming electrons experience much less attraction. Thus, the correct order is $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{I}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which noble gas has least tendency to form compounds?
questions.solution: Noble gas has least tendency to form compounds with helium due to highest ionisation enthalpy.
